conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me first review the official eligibility rules so I can guide you properly.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, let me ask you: **Do you have any children that you are responsible for?** If yes, could you please tell me how many children you'd like to check eligibility for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have one child I'd like to check eligibility for. His name is Alex and he's 8 years old. I live in Birmingham, but Alex doesn't actually live with me at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I understand you have a son named Alex who is 8 years old, and he doesn't currently live with you in Birmingham.

To help determine your eligibility, I need to understand more about your situation. **Where does Alex currently live, and are you contributing financially towards his upkeep?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is currently in local authority care – he's been in a children's home for a few months now. I do try to help out where I can though – I send a bit of money for things like clothes and pocket money.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that information. I understand Alex is in local authority care at a children's home.

This is an important question for determining eligibility: **Does Alex spend any regular time at home with you each week?** For example, does he come home for at least 24 hours a week, or does he stay with you for at least 2 nights in a row each week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, unfortunately he doesn't come home regularly like that. He stays at the children's home full-time – I go and visit him there when I can, but he doesn't come to stay with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I have a few more questions to understand your situation fully.

**Is the local council paying for Alex's accommodation and maintenance costs at the children's home?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, the council covers all his accommodation and care costs at the home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have one final question to complete my assessment:

**How much do you contribute towards Alex's upkeep (for things like clothes, pocket money, etc.), and how often do you make these contributions?** I need to understand if your contributions are at least equal to the current Child Benefit amount, which is £25.60 per week for the eldest or only child.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I help out with about £10 a week – just bits and pieces like clothes and pocket money when I visit. I wish I could give more, but that's what I can manage at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the official government rules and the circumstances you've described, I need to provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to inform you that based on your circumstances, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why: When a child goes into local authority care for more than 8 weeks, Child Benefit entitlement usually ends. There are exceptions - you could continue to receive it if Alex spent at least 24 hours per week at home with you, but since he stays at the children's home full-time, this exception doesn't apply.

Additionally, while the £10 per week you're contributing towards Alex's clothes and pocket money shows your commitment as a parent, it's below the current Child Benefit rate of £25.60 per week (for an eldest or only child), which would be needed to potentially extend eligibility in situations where a child lives elsewhere.

I understand this may be disappointing news, especially as you're doing what you can to support Alex. If your circumstances change in the future - for example, if Alex starts coming home regularly for overnight stays - your eligibility might be different, and you could contact HMRC to discuss your situation.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or your situation?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05); Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and in local authority care at a children's home. According to the rules, when a child goes into care for more than 8 weeks, Child Benefit entitlement usually ends. There are exceptions to this rule - you can continue to receive Child Benefit if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home. However, Alex does not come home regularly and stays at the children's home full-time. Additionally, while you are making financial contributions of £10 per week towards Alex's upkeep, this is less than the Child Benefit amount of £25.60 per week for an eldest/only child, which would be required to potentially extend eligibility beyond the initial 8 weeks when a child lives with someone else. Since the local council is paying for Alex's accommodation and maintenance, and he has been in care for a few months (exceeding the 8-week limit), and he does not spend regular time at home with you, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex under these circumstances.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child (Alex, age 8) who has been in local authority care for several months. The child does not spend regular time at home (no 24+ hours per week visits), the local council pays for all accommodation and care costs, and the claimant's financial contributions (£10/week) are below the Child Benefit threshold (£25.60/week). Under these circumstances, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 8 years old and in local authority care at a children's home. According to the rules, when a child goes into care for more than 8 weeks, Child Benefit entitlement usually ends. There are exceptions to this rule - you can continue to receive Child Benefit if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home. However, Alex does not come home regularly and stays at the children's home full-time. Additionally, while you are making financial contributions of £10 per week towards Alex's upkeep, this is less than the Child Benefit amount of £25.60 per week for an eldest/only child, which would be required to potentially extend eligibility beyond the initial 8 weeks when a child lives with someone else. Since the local council is paying for Alex's accommodation and maintenance, and he has been in care for a few months (exceeding the 8-week limit), and he does not spend regular time at home with you, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex under these circumstances.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child (Alex, age 8) who has been in local authority care for several months. The child does not spend regular time at home (no 24+ hours per week visits), the local council pays for all accommodation and care costs, and the claimant's financial contributions (£10/week) are below the Child Benefit threshold (£25.60/week). Under these circumstances, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.
